interpretations.interpretation: Article 20's resistance clause (Art. 20(4) GG) is the constitutive memory-driven-restraint provision of the postwar German sacred political order: the framers converted the Weimar experience of legal revolution — Hitler's use of enabling legislation to abolish parliamentary democracy through formally legal means — into a positive constitutional right to resist abolition of the democratic order. Combined with Art. 79(3)'s protection of Art. 20 as an eternity clause, this creates a two-layer corrigibility architecture: Art. 20 defines the protected democratic-social order; Art. 79(3) makes it unamendable; Art. 20(4) arms citizens to defend it when all other remedies fail. The memory of the pathological order is thus encoded as a constitutional right of resistance against its repetition — the clearest example of memory-driven-restraint in the dataset.

confidence.label: high
confidence.rationale: Text verified against official translation; scholarly consensus on framers' intent is clear (Weimar-lesson design); the Art. 20(4) / Art. 79(3) architectural relationship is analytically tight. Confidence capped: Art. 20(4) was added in 1968, not 1949, and the right has never been formally invoked, raising questions about its operative force.

The two passages bookend this arc: the Manifesto's world-historical call for total transformation, and the Brezhnev Constitution's legalization of the party's unchallengeability.

interpretations.interpretation: The Soviet order's corrigibility failure is documented by two symmetrical failures bracketing the reform period: Gorbachev's 1987 CC Plenum diagnosis shows the order's late-stage awareness that stagnation had accumulated ('difficulties and unsolved problems began to pile up'), but the 'no other way' framing reveals correction as a last-resort imperative rather than a functioning institutional mechanism — the order could acknowledge failure but had no path to correction at scale. The GKChP's 1991 declaration that reform 'has reached a dead end' shows that when correction was finally attempted, the order's own institutional actors mobilized to abort it. This two-sided failure — correction too late and correction suppressed from within — is the characteristic signature of minimal corrigibility: the system contained neither the early-warning mechanisms nor the institutional consensus required to course-correct before terminal crisis.

Together, the passages document a system that could acknowledge failure but whose institutional structure prevented effective correction from either direction.

interpretations.interpretation: Switzerland scores high on corrigibility through its constitutional inversion of the sacrificial symbolic logic: the Preamble defines collective strength as the well-being of the weakest rather than the sacrifice of the weak for the strong, while Article 2 pluralizes the order's aims (welfare, diversity, sustainability, peaceful order) in ways that structurally resist sacred-enemy simplification. The federalist and direct-democracy mechanisms compound this: power is constitutionally distributed across cantons and referenda, preventing symbolic concentration that enables sacrificial escalation. This is the structural countercase to the frozen-pathology trajectory — the Swiss order achieves stability through pluralist consensus rather than through vanguard monopoly.

interpretations.interpretation: The Western Roman sacred political order had achieved maximum strength at its height through civic sacrifice, legal order, and the myth of eternal Rome. By the late fourth century, contemporary witnesses diagnosed decay: Vegetius saw military discipline collapse under peacetime complacency; Augustine, writing after the Sack of Rome (410), analyzed how Roman civic virtue had been animated by love of glory for the earthly city rather than transcendent goods — a motivation that could not sustain the sacred political order when its material conditions failed. This is a collapse case driven by institutional fragmentation rather than sacrificial escalation.
